cases.title: Two Rescuers, One Infant
cases.presentation: A 4-month-old is found unresponsive and pulseless. You and a partner, both trained, respond with a bag-mask. You are able to encircle the infant's small chest with your hands.
cases.question: What compression technique and ratio should you use?
options: B: Two-thumb–encircling-hands technique at 15:2, depth about 1.5 inches, rate 100–120/min.
D: Heel of one hand at 5:1.
cases.explanation: With two rescuers who can encircle the chest, the preferred 2025 technique is two-thumb–encircling-hands at a 15:2 ratio, compressing the lower half of the sternum to about 1.5 inches (one-third the chest) at 100–120/min.
cases.clinicalPearl: Two thumbs, encircle the chest, 15:2 with a partner — the 2025 preferred infant approach.

cases.title: Alone With an Infant
cases.presentation: You are the only rescuer with an unresponsive, pulseless 6-month-old. Your hands are large enough to encircle the infant's chest. You did not witness the collapse.
cases.question: What technique should a lone rescuer use, and what is the sequence?
options: A: Two-finger technique, and leave immediately to find an AED.

B: Two-thumb–encircling-hands technique (since you can encircle), give about 2 minutes of CPR first for this unwitnessed collapse, then call for help and get an AED — or call on speaker while doing CPR.
C: Adult two-hand technique and shock first.
cases.explanation: The 2025 Guidelines prefer the two-thumb–encircling-hands technique even for a lone rescuer who can encircle the chest. For an unwitnessed infant collapse (likely hypoxic), provide about 2 minutes of CPR before leaving to activate help and get an AED, ideally using a speakerphone.
cases.clinicalPearl: The two-finger technique is retired — if you can encircle the chest, use two thumbs even when alone.

cases.title: Infant Bradycardia
cases.presentation: A 2-month-old with bronchiolitis becomes limp. You are ventilating with good chest rise and supplemental oxygen, but the brachial heart rate is 45/min and the baby is pale and unresponsive.
cases.question: What is the correct action?
options: A: Continue ventilation only and monitor.
B: Begin chest compressions because the heart rate is under 60 with poor perfusion despite ventilation.
C: Apply an AED and shock.
cases.explanation: An infant with a heart rate below 60/min and poor perfusion despite adequate oxygenation and ventilation should receive chest compressions, using the two-thumb–encircling-hands technique. A slow, poorly perfusing rhythm quickly progresses to full arrest.
cases.clinicalPearl: In babies and children alike, 'under 60 with poor perfusion despite good ventilation' means start compressions.
